enrollment.body: Each course is $300. All enrollment is handled personally by Nick. To reserve your place — or to be notified when the next course opens — reach out directly.
enrollment.right_body_1: To enroll or ask questions, email Unshelledcoast@gmail.com and indicate your preferred payment method.
enrollment.right_body_2: Include your name, any questions about the course, and whether you prefer live or asynchronous enrollment.

meta.title: Nick Rosado · Enlightenment Educator
meta.description: Live and asynchronous courses on Homer, Virgil, Dante, and Milton with Nick Rosado.
meta.og_description: Four poets. Four poems. One year. Live and asynchronous courses on the great epic poets.

about.pull_quote: I teach literature for the same reason Homer sang — because these stories matter, and the ancient flames of the heroes within are worthy of spending a lifetime fanning.
about.body_1: The great epic poems are not difficult because they are obscure — they are deep. Given the right context, the right structure, and the right companions for the journey, anyone can read them and be profoundly moved.
about.body_2: Each course combines pre-recorded lectures, weekly live sessions, and the kind of close attention to the text that most readers never get. Nick handles all enrollment personally.

accordion.title: Format & Expectations
accordion.content:
• 11 weekly live sessions, one hour each
  • Pre-recorded lectures for every Book, split into pre-reading and post-reading sections
  • Required text: Iliad, translated by E.V. Rieu
  • ~2–3 hours of total weekly commitment (lecture + reading + session)
  • All sessions recorded and shared via private link with passcode
  • Full asynchronous option available

accordion.title: How the Lectures Work
accordion.content:
Each pre-recorded lecture has two parts. Before you read, you listen to the introduction — essential context, background and what to look for. After you read, you return for the integrations section, which draws on copious poetic research and connects the Book to broader themes.

Recommended order: Introduction → Read the Book → Integrations.

accordion.title: Recurring Assignments
accordion.content:
• Weekly line selection: Each student chooses one line or short passage — one they love, hate or have a question about — and shares it during class with a brief explanation.
  • Plot summary: Two or three students per week each write a ~seven-sentence summary for one assigned Book, shared at the start of discussion.

accordion.title: Class Structure
accordion.content: Each Book gets the same three-part structure: (1) Student's short plot summary, (2) Instructor's one to two thematic focus points from the lecture, (3) All students' line selections and open discussion. After class, students have open access to the instructor for further questions.

accordion.title: Class 1: Orientation & Trojan War Context
accordion.content:
Pre-Class Viewing: The Trojan War Cycle (6 min) · Greek Gods Explained in 12 Minutes · Optional: Epic Cycle — The Lost Tales (12 min) · Optional film: Troy (2003)

During Class: Introduction to the epic poetry tradition, Homer's biography and historical context, the Trojan War cycle and key philosophical concepts in the Iliad.

accordion.title: Classes 2–11: Book-by-Book
accordion.content: Each subsequent class covers two (sometimes three) Books of the Iliad (Books 1–24 across 11 sessions). Pre-class work includes the pre-recorded lecture and reading assignment. During class: student summaries, instructor's thematic focus, and group line discussion.

featured.subtitle: Translated by E.V. Rieu · Instructor: Nick Rosado
featured.description:
The Iliad is not a poem primarily about war. It is a poem fundamentally about life and what makes it worth living — captured in grief, triumph, and in the choices that cannot be unmade. Homer's great insight is that his heroes know this, and choose glory anyway. We will sit with their choices for two and a half months.

Each week: a pre-recorded lecture to prepare and unpack the reading, a live one-hour session to discuss it together and the kind of close attention to the text that most readers never get. All sessions are recorded. Asynchronous enrollment is available.

cards.title: The Iliad
cards.description: Injustice, glory, grief and the price of life. The oldest epic in the Western tradition — read not as ancient history, but as the first western tragedy.

cards.title: The Aeneid
cards.description: Rome's founding epic — a meditation on duty, love and the price of empire through Aeneas, a hero who pursued greatness and paid for it.

cards.title: The Divine Comedy
cards.description: A journey through Hell and Purgatory. Inferno and Purgatorio fully built and taught — for readers who seek a true grasp of Dante.

cards.title: Paradise Lost
cards.description: The most ambitious poem in the English language. Satan, free will, the Fall and what it means to be human. We close the year with Milton.

hero.subtitle: Homer, Virgil, Dante, and Milton — four poets, four poems, one year. Small-group online courses built with the depth and attention these works deserve.

quote.text_line_1: Sing, goddess, the anger of Achilles —
quote.text_line_2: and the grief it brought upon the Greeks.
quote.attribution: Homer · Iliad · Book I · Opening Line
